title: applyDecodePolicy null-guard on `p` is unreachable due to TypeScript constraint but masks future runtime risk
description: The `if (!p)` guard at line 57 can never be true: `name` is typed as `DecodePolicyName` and `decodePolicies` is a `const satisfies Record<DecodePolicyName, DecodePolicy>`, so every valid name is always present. When callers bypass TypeScript (e.g. via `as any` or a dynamic string cast through `isDecodePolicyName`), the guard would only fire at runtime after a type-unsound call. The same pattern exists in `applyEncodePolicy` (line 94). While not a crash today, the guard gives false confidence: a caller that first checks `isDecodePolicyName` and then calls `applyDecodePolicy` can still pass an unknown name if they forget the guard, and the resulting error message is the only safety net. This is a low-severity latent issue but worth making explicit.

title: downsampleForContainer returns wrong result for non-integer or very large ratio due to truncating bitwise cast
description: `ratio | 0` truncates a float to a 32-bit signed integer. For extremely large images (e.g. imageW=4_294_967_296, containerW=1), `ratio` overflows a 32-bit int and `ratio | 0` becomes 0, causing `Math.clz32(0)` to return 32, so `log2 = 31 - 32 = -1`, and `1 << -1` evaluates to `1 << 31 = -2147483648` as a signed int. The cast `as Downsample` then returns a large negative number rather than clamping to 8. Real photographic images are unlikely to hit 4 billion pixels wide, but synthetic/test inputs can. The guard `imageW <= 0 || imageH <= 0` does not cover this. A safer implementation would use `Math.floor(Math.log2(ratio))` or clamp `ratio` before the bitwise op.

title: downsampleForContainer does not guard against NaN or Infinity inputs
description: `imageW <= 0` is false for `NaN` (NaN comparisons always return false), so passing `NaN` for any dimension bypasses the guard and proceeds into the ratio computation. `Math.min(NaN, ...)` returns NaN, `NaN < 2` is false, `NaN | 0` is 0, leading to `Math.clz32(0) = 32`, `log2 = -1`, and ultimately `1 << -1 = -2147483648` returned as `Downsample`. Similarly `Infinity / Infinity` produces NaN. The guard should be `!Number.isFinite(x) || x <= 0` for all four parameters.

title: applyDecodePolicy and applyEncodePolicy accept null base without throwing, silently spreading null
description: Both `applyDecodePolicy` and `applyEncodePolicy` spread `base` using `{ ...base, ... }`. If a caller passes `null` or `undefined` as `base` (possible via `as any` or a dynamic dispatch path), spreading `null` in JavaScript silently produces an empty object (`{ ...null }` is `{}`), so the function does not throw but returns a policy-only object without the caller's intended fields. The TypeScript type prevents this at compile-time, but runtime callers (e.g. from plain JS or dynamic invocation) get silent data loss. A `if (base == null) throw new TypeError(...)` guard would surface the mistake.

title: Policy errors use bare RangeError with no error code, making programmatic handling harder for callers
description: Both `applyDecodePolicy` and `applyEncodePolicy` throw `new RangeError(message)` with a human-readable string. Callers that need to distinguish a policy-name error from other RangeErrors (e.g. from downstream decode/encode option validation) must parse the message string, which is fragile. A sentinel subclass (e.g. `class PolicyNameError extends RangeError { code = 'ERR_UNKNOWN_POLICY' }`) or at minimum a `code` property would let callers branch reliably without string matching.

title: downsampleForContainer tests do not cover NaN or very-large-ratio inputs that cause integer overflow
description: The helper math tests (cases 1-6) verify zero/negative guard and normal ratios, but there is no test for NaN inputs or for ratios large enough to overflow `ratio | 0` (> 2^31). These are the exact inputs that expose the integer-overflow and NaN-propagation bugs identified in errors-2 and errors-3. Adding these cases would both document the expected behavior (clamp to 8) and catch any regression if the implementation changes.
